lectures.text:
The gradient of a differentiable scalar function is the vector of its partial derivatives. In coordinates, the gradient formula is

\[
\nabla f(x) = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\ldots,\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_d}\right).
\]

The gradient is a mathematical concept, while the coordinate expression is a Formula that formalizes it.

Maximum-Likelihood Estimation estimates an econometric model by maximizing the likelihood of the observed sample. The likelihood is viewed as a function of the model parameters with the sample held fixed.
The degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to that vertex in an undirected graph. A vertex is an element of the graph's vertex set.

MLE is used to fit a probabilistic model by maximizing its likelihood function on observed training data. The likelihood function assigns each candidate parameter value a score determined by the fixed observations.

An ODE describes a function through derivatives with respect to one independent variable. Euler's Method approximates its solution by repeatedly applying

\[
y_{n+1}=y_n+h f(t_n,y_n).
\]

An ordinary differential equation relates an unknown function of one variable to its derivatives. The Forward Euler Method advances an approximate solution using the current slope. Its update is

\[
y_{n+1}=y_n+h f(t_n,y_n).
\]

The gradient vector points in the direction of steepest local increase of a differentiable objective. Gradient descent is an iterative Method that moves in the opposite direction of this gradient vector to reduce the objective.
The degree of a nonzero polynomial is the largest exponent whose coefficient is nonzero. A polynomial is a finite linear combination of nonnegative integer powers of its variable.

Maximum Likelihood Estimation selects parameter values that maximize the likelihood. The likelihood compares parameter values after the observed data are held fixed. An MLE estimator may be written as

\[
\widehat{\theta}_{\mathrm{MLE}} = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\theta} L(\theta;x).
\]
